item.title: (1978) Ambient 1: Music for Airports
item.description: “1/1” began as an accident Brian Eno found on playback: he had set a small group improvising exercises in a London studio, the musicians barely able to hear one another, and somewhere in the reel two pianos, unbeknownst to each other, had played melodic lines that interlocked.

item.title: Classical music
item.description: In 1829, the English organist Vincent Novello wrote in his diary of a Continental chapel that it was the place “I should come to every Sunday when I wished to hear classical music correctly and judiciously performed” — the first sentence the Oxford English Dictionary can find with the family’s name...

item.title: Dionne Warwick
item.description: Listen to the way Dionne Warwick navigates the melody of “Walk on By” — the intervals are wide, and the phrasing syncopates against Burt Bacharach’s rhythm track in ways that make the emotional content of the lyric (heartbreak, composure, the effort of pretending not to care) feel inevitable rather...

item.title: Phil Spector
item.description: Phil Spector is the first pop producer to matter as an auteur, the first to make the argument, through sheer sonic force, that the producer could be the primary author of a record.

item.title: Paul McCartney
item.description: Paul McCartney plays the bass like a second singer.

item.title: The Funk Brothers
item.description: The most prolific and least credited ensemble in the history of recorded music.

item.title: The Wrecking Crew
item.description: The loose collective of Los Angeles session musicians who played on more hit records in the 1960s than any other group of players in any city in the world.

item.title: Burt Bacharach
item.description: Burt Bacharach is the most harmonically sophisticated songwriter in the history of American pop — the figure who brought Ravel, Debussy, and Brazilian bossa nova into the three-minute single without sacrificing emotional accessibility.

item.title: Gerry Goffin
item.description: Gerry Goffin was the lyricist of the most prolific and versatile songwriting team in the Brill Building — the partner who gave Carole King’s melodies their words and, more importantly, their emotional intelligence.

item.title: The Ronettes
item.description: The Ronettes are the girl group at its most potent, the act that proved what happened when Brill Building craft and Phil Spector’s Wall of Sound met a voice strong enough to ride them both.
